content:
Wrapping a component in @defer doesn’t mean Angular defers it. Sometimes it quietly does nothing and sometimes it makes your metrics worse. Here’s what six months of using it properly looks like.

5 min read
2 hours ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

The first time I used @defer, it did nothing.
Not “broke something” nothing. I wrapped a heavy chart component, rebuilt, and the main bundle was byte-for-byte the same size. No error, no warning. The component loaded eagerly like it always had, and if I hadn’t checked the build output, I’d have shipped a template that looks optimized and isn’t.
That’s the thing about @defer: the syntax is trivial and the behavior has rules nobody reads. So this is the article I wanted that day the patterns that actually move the numbers, and the three ways the feature quietly turns against you.
The two rules that decide everything
For Angular to actually defer a component, two conditions must hold:

• It’s standalone. NgModule-declared components can’t be deferred. They load eagerly, inside your @defer block, without a word of complaint.
